Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings. With 20 online courses to manage, I had to be pretty systematic about it. I'd make lists of what needed to be done each week and check things off as I went. It wasn't exciting, but it kept me from falling too far behind. I mostly stuck to reading materials and taking notes since that's how I learn best.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but honestly deadlines often disrupted that. When multiple assignments were due, I'd have to cram more hours in, which was stressful given everything else going on. My attendance wasn't perfect at 66% because sometimes life just got in the way. I tried my best to maintain the routine, but it definitely varied more than I would have liked.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing when things were due. I'd also log in to watch required videos or access reading materials. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of interesting, so sometimes I'd check in to see my progress on those. Fear of missing something important was probably my biggest motivator to log in regularly.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic, like checking email. The gamified elements helped make it feel less like a chore because I could see my progress. I'd log in, see what badges or points I'd earned, and then get to work. It became part of my morning routine before I started my actual studying.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and take detailed notes from them. The videos were okay, but I often found myself rewinding and pausing to write things down. The task completion tracking was helpful because it showed me exactly what I still needed to do.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums, posting only once the whole course. I'm not comfortable putting my thoughts out there for everyone to see, and with limited resources, I couldn't always be online to have back-and-forth conversations. Some of the optional supplementary materials I skipped because I was already stretched thin just completing the required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. I couldn't afford to waste time on things that wouldn't help my grade. The gamified tasks were a nice break because they made progress feel tangible, but ultimately I prioritized anything that would show up on assessments. With limited time and resources, I had to be strategic.
Q8: Required activities got done no matter what because I was scared of falling behind or failing. Optional ones felt like luxuries I couldn't really afford. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional readings, but I had to prioritize. The stress of keeping up with requirements meant optional things usually got pushed aside unless they seemed directly beneficial.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and study from printed notes when I could afford to print, or from handwritten notes. I spent hours reviewing my own written summaries that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. A lot of my learning happened away from my computer entirely.
Q10: My best studying was often me sitting with my notebook, re-reading my handwritten notes and testing myself on concepts. None of that would show up in the LMS. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then study my notes from it for hours afterward. The system would only show that one video view, not the extensive review that followed.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download readings the first time I accessed them, then study from the downloaded files for days. I'd access a video, take detailed notes, and then never watch it again because I had my notes. The LMS would show minimal activity, but I was actually engaging deeply with the material offline.
Q12: There were weeks when I felt completely overwhelmed and scared about keeping up. My stress levels were really high, and sometimes I'd just freeze up. When multiple deadlines converged, I'd feel paralyzed about where to start. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me hard and made it difficult to re-engage.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, using the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. Sometimes I'd log in but my brain just couldn't process anything meaningful. Competing responsibilities meant I had to rush through materials rather than really absorbing them. Fatigue made everything take longer than it should have.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere. I was going through the motions to check things off my list without really learning. When I was scared about upcoming deadlines, I'd access things frantically without actually engaging deeply. Those sessions felt empty even though they generated activity data.
Q15: I'd sometimes put off discussion posts because they made me anxious. Even knowing participation mattered, the fear of saying something wrong held me back. I also postponed starting difficult assignments because I was scared of not understanding the material. The procrastination usually made my stress worse, but I couldn't always help it.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd become much more active in the days before something was due. My login frequency spiked around due dates and dropped off afterward. It wasn't ideal, but the fear of missing deadlines was a powerful motivator that kept me completing assignments even when I felt overwhelmed.
Q17: Feedback was important but also scary to receive. Good feedback motivated me to keep going with my approach. Critical feedback sometimes made me anxious about the next assignment. I tried to use feedback to improve, but honestly sometimes I was too stressed to fully process it before moving on to the next deadline.
Q18: The structured deadlines and clear requirements helped me know exactly what to do, which I needed. The gamified elements gave me small wins that kept me going when everything felt overwhelming. Having 20 courses meant the structure had to be clear or I'd get lost. The predictability of the course design helped me maintain my routine.
Q19: The clear task lists and completion tracking made engagement easier because I always knew where I stood. The gamified badges gave me something positive to focus on when I was stressed. However, the discussion requirements were harder for me because I'm not comfortable with public participation. Having so many courses made it hard to engage deeply with any single one.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding, clicking through materials trying to make sense of things but struggling to produce work. Or they could be anxious like me, checking and rechecking things without actually completing assignments. High activity might also mean someone is lost and looking for help they're not finding.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I performed reasonably well but my engagement level was low according to the system. I did a lot of learning offline that didn't register. Someone who knows how to study efficiently might not need to spend as much time in the LMS to understand the material and complete assignments well.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone is active and what they access. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I actually understood something or just clicked through it. They definitely miss offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone is truly learning or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely trying to learn something rather than just checking boxes. For me, engagement is sitting with my notes and really working through concepts, even when it's hard. It's more about mental investment than visible activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I often felt, or how much stress affected my ability to engage meaningfully. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes away from my computer. It can't show that I was highly motivated despite appearing to have low engagement, or that I was doing my best with limited resources. My internal experience of learning is invisible to the system.
